notes.purpose: Student-friendly notes based on linked video lessons and available transcript checks. Full video transcripts are not stored.
notes.transcriptPolicy: Use transcript availability to guide lesson improvement; avoid copying full copyrighted scripts into the app.

Place Value.title: Math Antics - Place Value
keyNotes: Digits get their value from their position, not just their shape.
Read large numbers by grouping digits into ones, thousands, millions, and so on.
Zero is a place holder when a column has no value.
Exam practice: underline a digit, name its column, then write its value.
Place Value.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Place Value in your own words, then add one example.

Angles.title: Types of Angles | Math with Mr. J
keyNotes: An angle measures the amount of turn between two lines or rays.
Acute angles are smaller than 90 degrees; right angles are exactly 90 degrees.
Obtuse angles are between 90 and 180 degrees; a straight angle is 180 degrees.
Exam practice: estimate first, then classify the angle using its measurement.
Angles.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Angles in your own words, then add one example.

Subtraction / Addition.title: Math Antics - Multi-Digit Subtraction
keyNotes: Line up digits by place value before adding or subtracting.
Addition may require carrying into the next column.
Subtraction may require borrowing from the next column.
Check word problems by deciding whether the situation is joining, increasing, taking away, or comparing.
Subtraction / Addition.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Subtraction / Addition in your own words, then add one example.

Multiplying.title: Math Antics - Multi-Digit Multiplication Pt 1
keyNotes: Multiplication means equal groups and repeated addition.
For two-digit multiplication, multiply by the ones, then by the tens, then add the partial products.
A zero placeholder keeps tens work in the correct column.
Exam practice: show working clearly so the marker can follow each step.
Multiplying.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Multiplying in your own words, then add one example.

Area / Perimeter.title: Perimeter, Area, and Volume Explained | Math with Mr. J
keyNotes: Perimeter is the distance around the outside edge of a shape.
Area is the amount of space inside a flat shape.
Rectangles use perimeter = length + width + length + width and area = length x width.
Use square units for area and ordinary length units for perimeter.
Area / Perimeter.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Area / Perimeter in your own words, then add one example.

Number Pattern.title: Math Antics - Number Patterns
keyNotes: A number pattern follows a rule.
Compare neighbouring numbers to find whether the rule adds, subtracts, multiplies, or divides.
Test the rule on more than one step before using it.
Exam practice: write the rule and then the missing or next number.
Number Pattern.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Number Pattern in your own words, then add one example.

Novel.title: Grade 6 English - Analyzing Story Plot
keyNotes: Plot means the sequence of story events from beginning to ending.
Characters change or reveal traits through actions, speech, and choices.
Setting affects what characters can do and how the mood feels.
Short answers need a claim plus evidence from the passage.
Novel.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Novel in your own words, then add one example.

Media Explosion.title: English 6 - Infer the Purpose of Visual Media
keyNotes: Visual media uses pictures, layout, colour, headings, and short text to communicate quickly.
Purpose may be to inform, persuade, warn, entertain, or sell.
Audience means the people the message is made for.
Exam practice: identify the audience, purpose, and one visual clue.
Media Explosion.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Media Explosion in your own words, then add one example.

Grammar (Noun, Verb).title: Basic English Grammar - Noun, Verb, Adjective, Adverb
keyNotes: Nouns name people, places, things, or ideas.
Verbs show action or being, and they must match tense and subject.
Adjectives describe nouns; adverbs describe verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs.
Exam practice: label parts of speech, then use them correctly in your own sentence.
Grammar (Noun, Verb).practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Grammar (Noun, Verb) in your own words, then add one example.

Vocabulary.title: Vocabulary Mastery - Grade 6 English Lesson
keyNotes: A synonym has a similar meaning; an antonym has an opposite meaning.
Context clues can be examples, contrasts, actions, results, or descriptions.
Word parts like prefixes and suffixes can help unlock meaning.
Short answers should explain the meaning and name the clue.
Vocabulary.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Vocabulary in your own words, then add one example.

Reading Comprehension.title: Reading Comprehension Skills and Strategies
keyNotes: Read once for the main idea and again for important details.
Literal answers are directly stated in the text.
Inference answers combine text clues with sensible thinking.
Use evidence words such as because, the text says, and this shows.
Reading Comprehension.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Reading Comprehension in your own words, then add one example.

Punctuation.title: Grade 6 English - Punctuation
keyNotes: Capital letters begin sentences and proper nouns.
Full stops, question marks, and exclamation marks show sentence endings.
Commas separate items in a list and help readers pause.
Speech marks show the exact words a speaker says.
Punctuation.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Punctuation in your own words, then add one example.

Geographers Tools.title: The Geographer's Tools
keyNotes: Geographers use tools to observe, measure, record, and explain places.
A compass shows direction; a map shows location and features.
Weather tools include thermometers for temperature and barometers for air pressure.
Exam practice: name the tool and explain what it measures or shows.
Geographers Tools.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Geographers Tools in your own words, then add one example.

Representing Geography.title: Grade 6 Social Sciences Geography - Atlas
keyNotes: Maps represent real places using scale, symbols, colour, direction, and keys.
A legend explains symbols; a scale converts map distance into real distance.
An atlas is a collection of maps.
Exam practice: read the title, key, direction, and scale before answering.
Representing Geography.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Representing Geography in your own words, then add one example.

Creating Maps of Vanuatu.title: Maps for Kids | How to Read a Map
keyNotes: Vanuatu is made of many islands grouped into provinces.
Maps can show islands, towns, coastlines, provinces, and compass direction.
Good maps need a title, key, scale, labels, and north arrow.
Exam practice: locate provinces and major towns, then describe direction using north, south, east, and west.
Creating Maps of Vanuatu.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Creating Maps of Vanuatu in your own words, then add one example.

The New Hebrides Condominium.title: The Condominium of the New Hebrides: A Brief History of Vanuatu
keyNotes: Before independence, Vanuatu was called the New Hebrides.
Britain and France ruled together under the Condominium.
Dual rule created two systems of government, law, language, and money.
Exam practice: explain why this arrangement was confusing for local people.
The New Hebrides Condominium.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining The New Hebrides Condominium in your own words, then add one example.

Road to Independence.title: History of Vanuatu Independence
keyNotes: Independence means a country governs itself and makes its own decisions.
Vanuatu became independent on 30 July 1980.
National symbols such as the flag and anthem help show shared identity.
Exam practice: connect dates, names, and symbols to the idea of self-government.
Road to Independence.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Road to Independence in your own words, then add one example.

Origin of Culture / Protecting our Herit.title: What Is Cultural Heritage?
keyNotes: Culture includes language, custom, stories, dance, art, food, beliefs, and ceremonies.
Heritage is what people protect and pass to the next generation.
Protecting heritage means learning it, respecting it, and teaching it to young people.
Exam practice: give a local example and explain how people can protect it.
Origin of Culture / Protecting our Herit.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Origin of Culture / Protecting our Heritage in your own words, then add one example.

Preserve the Environment.title: Ecosystem and Nature Conservation | Dr Binocs
keyNotes: The environment includes land, sea, air, plants, animals, and people.
Conservation means using resources wisely so they remain for the future.
Tabu areas can protect fish, forests, reefs, and wildlife.
Exam practice: name a problem, a cause, and a practical solution.
Preserve the Environment.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Preserve the Environment in your own words, then add one example.

Vanuatu National Symbols, Geography & Tr.title: Vanuatu Explained in 15 Minutes | History, Geography, Culture
keyNotes: Vanuatu is a Y-shaped archipelago of about 83 islands in the South Pacific.
The country sits on the Pacific Ring of Fire, giving it active volcanoes and frequent earthquakes.
The national anthem 'Yumi, Yumi, Yumi' was written by François Vincent Ayssav.
The national motto 'Long God yumi stanap' (In God we stand) was developed by Iawawoi Rezel Samana.
The flag colors are Red (blood unity), Black (Melanesian people), Green (land/agriculture), and Yellow (Christianity light).
The boar's tusk and namele leaves on the flag represent prosperity and peace.
Nagol (land diving) on Pentecost Island is a male-only ritual — a test of bravery with strict custom taboos preventing women from jumping.
Aneityum had unique funeral practices: high chiefs buried under house floors with heads exposed for later veneration.
Vanuatu National Symbols, Geography & Tr.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences about what makes Vanuatu's geography special, then name one national symbol and explain its meaning.

Food and Drinking Packaging.title: Traffic Lights and Food Packaging
keyNotes: Food packaging can protect food, but students must read labels carefully.
Important label information includes expiry date, ingredients, sugar, salt, fat, and storage advice.
Damaged, dirty, or expired packaging can be unsafe.
Exam practice: inspect the label before deciding whether food is healthy or safe.
Food and Drinking Packaging.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Food and Drinking Packaging in your own words, then add one example.

Fresh Food.title: Healthy and Unhealthy Foods for Kids
keyNotes: Fresh foods are often less processed and can provide vitamins, minerals, fibre, and energy.
Fruits and vegetables help protect the body.
Local fresh foods can support health and family gardens.
Exam practice: compare fresh food with packaged food using health reasons.
Fresh Food.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Fresh Food in your own words, then add one example.

Making Choices - What and Where.title: Making Healthy Choices
keyNotes: Healthy choices depend on what is eaten, where it comes from, and how it is prepared.
Choose clean water and fresh foods more often than sweet drinks and highly processed foods.
Food choices can be affected by price, availability, family habits, and advertising.
Exam practice: explain a healthy choice and give a reason.
Making Choices - What and Where.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Making Choices - What and Where in your own words, then add one example.

Special Food Needs.title: Eating Healthy with Nurse Deon
keyNotes: Different people need different foods at different stages of life.
Babies, pregnant mothers, sick people, elderly people, and active children may need special care.
Food allergies, illness, and body needs can change what is safe or helpful to eat.
Exam practice: match the person to the food need and explain why.
Special Food Needs.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Special Food Needs in your own words, then add one example.

Keep it Balanced.title: Wellbeing for Children: Healthy Habits
keyNotes: A balanced lifestyle includes good food, clean water, sleep, exercise, hygiene, and rest.
No single food gives everything the body needs.
Too much sugar, salt, or processed food can harm health over time.
Exam practice: build a balanced day plan, not just one meal.
Keep it Balanced.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Keep it Balanced in your own words, then add one example.

Clean Drinking Water.title: Health Lesson: Clean Drinking Water
keyNotes: Clean drinking water helps prevent sickness.
Water can be made safer by boiling, filtering, treating, and storing it in a clean covered container.
Dirty hands or dirty cups can make clean water unsafe again.
Exam practice: explain both how to make water safe and how to keep it safe.
Clean Drinking Water.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Clean Drinking Water in your own words, then add one example.

Immunisation.title: Why Do We Get Vaccines?
keyNotes: Immunisation helps protect the body before a disease attacks.
Vaccines train the immune system to recognise harmful germs.
High immunisation helps protect families and communities.
Exam practice: explain prevention, not just treatment.
Immunisation.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Immunisation in your own words, then add one example.

Malaria.title: What Causes Malaria? | Dr Binocs
keyNotes: Malaria is spread by infected mosquitoes.
Symptoms can include fever, chills, headache, tiredness, and body aches.
Prevention includes mosquito nets, removing standing water, and seeking treatment early.
Exam practice: explain cause, spread, symptoms, and prevention.
Malaria.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Malaria in your own words, then add one example.

Diabetes.title: What Causes Diabetes? | Dr Binocs
keyNotes: Diabetes is linked to problems controlling blood sugar.
Insulin helps control blood sugar in the body.
Healthy food, exercise, water, and medical care help manage risk.
Exam practice: explain lifestyle choices that reduce risk.
Diabetes.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Diabetes in your own words, then add one example.

Disease.title: What Is Disease? Types of Illnesses Explained for Kids
keyNotes: Diseases can be infectious or non-infectious.
Infectious diseases can spread through air, water, food, insects, or touch.
Prevention includes hygiene, safe water, immunisation, mosquito control, and healthy living.
Exam practice: name the disease type, how it spreads, and how to prevent it.
Disease.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Disease in your own words, then add one example.

What is Compost.title: Composting for Kids
keyNotes: Compost is decayed organic material that becomes rich soil.
Organic waste includes leaves, grass, vegetable scraps, and other once-living materials.
Plastic, glass, metal, and batteries do not belong in compost.
Exam practice: list what can go in compost and what must stay out.
What is Compost.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining What is Compost in your own words, then add one example.

How Composting Process Works.title: How Compost Is Made: A Field Trip
keyNotes: Composting needs air, moisture, warmth, and decomposers.
Brown materials add carbon; green materials add nitrogen.
Turning the pile adds oxygen and helps materials break down evenly.
Exam practice: explain the process from waste to useful soil.
How Composting Process Works.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining How Composting Process Works in your own words, then add one example.

Pest.title: Pest Control | Ecology and Environment
keyNotes: A pest is an organism that damages crops, stored food, homes, or health.
Pests can be controlled by hand-picking, barriers, companion plants, natural predators, and safe hygiene.
Not every insect is a pest; some are helpful.
Exam practice: identify the pest, the damage, and a safe control method.
Pest.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Pest in your own words, then add one example.

Friendly Organisms.title: Worms - Natural Science for Kids
keyNotes: Friendly organisms help gardens and ecosystems.
Earthworms loosen soil and add nutrients; bees help pollinate flowers.
Ladybugs and other predators can eat pests.
Exam practice: name the organism and explain its benefit.
Friendly Organisms.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Friendly Organisms in your own words, then add one example.

Living Things and the Environment.title: Basic Needs of Living Things | Science for Kids
keyNotes: Living things need food, water, air, shelter, and space.
A habitat includes biotic living parts and abiotic non-living parts.
Adaptations help organisms survive in their habitat.
Exam practice: connect a feature of a living thing to how it helps survival.
Living Things and the Environment.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Living Things and the Environment in your own words, then add one example.

Food Chain and Food Web.title: Food Chains - How Energy Moves Through Ecosystems
keyNotes: Producers make food, usually using sunlight.
Primary consumers eat producers; secondary consumers eat primary consumers.
Arrows show the direction energy moves, from food to eater.
Food webs show many connected food chains.
Food Chain and Food Web.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Food Chain and Food Web in your own words, then add one example.

Photosynthesis.title: Photosynthesis | Educational Video for Kids
keyNotes: Plants use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide to make glucose.
Chlorophyll in leaves captures light energy.
Oxygen is released during photosynthesis.
Exam practice: name the inputs and outputs clearly.
Photosynthesis.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Photosynthesis in your own words, then add one example.

Flowering Plants.title: Life Cycle of Flowering Plants | Science For Kids
keyNotes: Flowers help plants reproduce.
The stamen makes pollen; the pistil includes the stigma, style, and ovary.
Pollination moves pollen, often by wind or animals.
After pollination, seeds and fruit can form.
Flowering Plants.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Flowering Plants in your own words, then add one example.

Respiratory and Circulatory System.title: Respiratory and Circulatory Systems for Kids
keyNotes: The respiratory system brings oxygen into the body and removes carbon dioxide.
The circulatory system uses the heart, blood, arteries, veins, and capillaries to move materials.
Arteries carry blood away from the heart; veins carry blood back.
Exam practice: explain how lungs, heart, and blood work together.
Respiratory and Circulatory System.practicePrompt: After watching, write 3-4 sentences explaining Respiratory and Circulatory System in your own words, then add one example.
